topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The receptionist’s smile felt stretched thin, a fragile veneer over something brittle inside me. I clutched the strap of my worn messenger bag so tightly my knuckles were white. This was it: the first session with Marcus Thorne. He, the architect of my early misery, now sat behind a mahogany desk, radiating a calm authority that made my stomach clench. Every time he made eye contact, a familiar tightening started in my chest, a frantic fluttering that made my breath catch in my throat. I kept my gaze fixed on the polished wood grain of the desk, trying to regulate the rapid thump of my heart against my ribs. "So," Marcus began, his voice a low, even baritone, "tell me what brought you in today." My mouth felt dry, a sudden desert where words refused to form. I swallowed hard, the movement visible in my neck, and managed a whisper that barely cleared my vocal cords.

[story 2]

The fluorescent lights of the waiting room hummed with an irritating, high-pitched whine that seemed to vibrate directly through my bones. I kept shifting in my seat, adjusting my posture with jerky, unnecessary movements, as if movement could somehow anchor me to the floor. When the door opened, Marcus emerged. He looked… composed. The same sharp angles of his jaw, the way he held himself—it was instantly unsettling. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my skin prickle. I tried to appear relaxed, leaning back against the uncomfortable vinyl, but my hands were balled into fists in my lap, the tension radiating outward. "Hello," he said, his tone perfectly modulated, utterly devoid of the mocking edge I remembered so vividly. I managed a small nod, my eyes darting around the room, cataloging exits and escape routes, desperate to find a seam in the fabric of this unfamiliar, controlled space.

[story 3]

I paced the perimeter of the consultation room, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the tile floor. Each step felt deliberate, a conscious effort to control the tremor running through my limbs. When Marcus finally settled into his chair, I stood near the window, watching the distant traffic flow, unable to meet his gaze. The silence stretched, thick and heavy, pressing down on my shoulders. I could feel the pressure building behind my eyes, a desperate need to flee the immediate reality of the interaction. When he finally spoke, his voice was soft, almost gentle, which somehow made my internal alarm bells ring louder. "It takes courage to be here," he observed. I flinched, a small, involuntary jerk of my shoulders, my entire body tensing up as if bracing for impact. I squeezed my eyes shut for a brief second, fighting the urge to bolt from the room entirely.
